clean_story:
Pure energy. No mass.

The word light comes from the Old English lēoht, meaning "brightness"—but it’s far more than what eyes detect. Light is a wave of electric and magnetic fields, rippling like a pond’s surface after a dropped pebble, yet moving 670 million miles per hour. A single photon (a "particle" of light) takes just 1.28 seconds to travel from the moon to your eye.

Sunlight appears white, but prisms split it into colors because each wavelength bends differently—like runners on a track taking separate lanes. Red light crawls at 430 trillion waves per second; violet sprints at 750 trillion. Plants exploit this: chlorophyll absorbs red and blue, reflecting green, which is why leaves look that way.

Lasers prove light’s precision. A DVD player’s laser reads pits 0.4 microns wide—smaller than a bacterium—by detecting how light scatters off them. Meanwhile, fiber-optic cables trap light in glass threads thinner than hair, bouncing it along like a ball in a hallway of mirrors, carrying 10 terabits of data per second.

Three related subjects are color without pigments, how eyes deceive brains, and light as a cosmic ruler.
